reforms.description: The Slovak authorities have revised the R&D tax credit scheme to make it more accessible for firms, particularly small and micro enterprises, by removing high eligibility thresholds. Additionally, they are considering widening the R&D tax credit for firms in less developed regions to hire researchers or engineers and make the tax credit refundable for firms that do not yet make profits. Furthermore, the government plans to develop innovation vouchers to promote knowledge diffusion, allowing firms to consult innovation service providers to identify and implement technological projects.
reforms.package_name: R&D Tax Credit and Innovation Support
reforms.component_name: R&D Tax Credit and Innovation Vouchers
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The reforms aim to enhance access to R&D funding and support innovation, which can lead to increased productivity and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The R&D tax credit scheme was revised and implemented in 2013.
reforms.importance_rationale: The revisions to the R&D tax credit scheme and the introduction of innovation vouchers represent significant changes in the innovation support framework.
reforms.source_quote: The authorities could therefore give more consideration to widening the R&D tax credit for firms in less developed regions to hire researchers or engineers and make the tax credit refundable for firms which not yet make profits.

reforms.description: The Slovak government is promoting clusters in lagging regions to improve knowledge and technological transfers between firms and create a local labor market. Additionally, start-up incentives have been reformed to better adapt to local needs, with stricter eligibility criteria and a two-stage funding process to enhance effectiveness.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: These initiatives aim to foster innovation and entrepreneurship, which can stimulate economic growth and job creation.
reforms.status_evidence: Start-up incentives were reformed and implemented in 2013.
reforms.importance_rationale: The promotion of clusters and reform of start-up incentives are significant but not structural changes.
reforms.source_quote: Start-up incentives have been reduced, in line with the recommendations made in the previous Economic Survey, and reformed to better adapt to local needs.

reforms.description: The Slovak government aims to increase public investment in research and development, which has been low by OECD standards, and to concentrate state support for R&D on selected priority areas where Slovakia has a comparative advantage.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Increasing public investment in R&D is expected to enhance innovation capacity and economic competitiveness.
reforms.status_evidence: The government has announced plans to increase public investment in R&D.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant policy change aimed at boosting public R&D funding.
reforms.source_quote: The 2013 National Reform Programme sets 2020 targets for gross domestic expenditure on R&D and business expenditure on research and development at 1.2% and 0.8% respectively.

reforms.description: The Slovak authorities plan to provide tax exemptions to companies that offer workplace training to incentivize skills development in the labor market. Additionally, the government aims to increase public expenditure on education to 6% of GDP by 2020, aligning with the EU average, to improve educational outcomes and reduce inequalities. They are also considering the development of short tertiary programmes that include on-the-job training and employable skills to better prepare students for the labor market and technological change.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Investing in education and skills development is crucial for enhancing human capital, which supports innovation and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The government has announced plans to increase public expenditure on education and provide tax exemptions for workplace training.
reforms.importance_rationale: The increase in public expenditure on education and the introduction of tax exemptions for workplace training represent significant reforms in human capital policy.
reforms.source_quote: Developing short tertiary programmes, as currently considered by the authorities, that include on-the-job training and employable skills could provide the workers needed to use new technology.
